item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I like this in a weird way. It wasn't exciting, super catchy, gripping, or suspenseful. I felt like I was reading a very formal document on an SCP that isn't really all that special.

They seem to lack dangerous properties or anything in that book. If you think about it, it is rather sad. They have "personality and life" but are, at the end of the day, merely puppets; rejects of their kind even.

This SCP isn't scary, funny, witty, or anything like that in my opinion but that's ok. Not everything in the foundation should be dangerous or really over the top. I truly like this.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback. I'm not opposed to the idea of writing more about 1635, I just don't have any idea what to write. I could reveal more of the story behind this SCP - who makes them, what they do, and that kinda thing, but I think that would serve only to detract from the article. If you want to write something, though, go ahead!

The language is not specified, really: the reader can decide for herself what languages will be spoken in the 4th millenium. Personally, I like to think of it as being like his name: a mish-mash of lots of different languages and cultures, based on vaguely English and Chinese roots but with a huge number of borrowed words - and, of course, shifting pronunciation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's me hoping that things have settled down for you over the past month because I would be ecstatic if you wrote more about this SCP. But if not, I would still be happy just to have you answer this question I have:

What language does the boy who claims to be from the 3100s speak? Is it contemporary? Languages evolve, and though they do so a bit more slowly when there are dictionaries and widespread literacy and all that, I think we can still expect any language to change more or less dramatically between now and then. It seems to me the Foundation could use this as evidence for or against the boy's claim.

Anyway, I think it's a great article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, thanks for the praise! I really enjoyed writing this one, so I'm glad you enjoyed reading it.

I might consider writing more about 1635 one day, but at the moment I have lots going on at work, so I don't really have the time to even read SCPs any more, let alone write. Maybe once this all settles down a bit!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I just wanted to say "well done"; and I really enjoyed this entry. I found the idea really interesting, actually.

I don't really understand why this is isn't more widely liked - but then again, I'm a psychology buff and would love to study people like this. :)

I'd really like to see a Tale about 1635, or even more extensive interviews and such.

Anyways, you have my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was expecting a bit more, but it didn't fly for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted in the end. Just sayin'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, thanks Peppers! I'll try my hardest to come up with a great idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted this because I think it's just interesting enough to deserve a place on the site. It's a good article. Not great, but good. And that's okay.

Still, I'd encourage the author to really try to personally challenge themself when coming up with the concept for their next article, since I think that a stronger base idea with the author's writing ability could very likely produce a great skip, if not better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, see. He's Anglo-Saxon… kinda. Harold is a perfectly reasonable Anglo-Saxon name (see: Harold Godwinson, the monarch who died at the battle of Hastins). As for the surname - this man is the son of a Nordic settler. Hence his being the son of Erik. His mother is Anglo-Saxon and he grew up amongst Anglo-Saxons, so he speaks their language: Old English.

I'll admit to making this backstory up because I couldn't find information on common Anglo Saxon surnames. It could be horribly anachronistic.

As for variety in the folks: the company is registered in (some version of) the UK, and as their services presumably cost a lot of money, and the people with lots of money in the UK tend to be relatively similar… *shrug*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Abnormally high is still statistically significant - just very statistically significant. I wanted to imply that they're not visible by any means, but boiling up a big soup of the neural matter and then doing … hmm … mass spectroscopy? Something like that? indicates consideraby more gold than normal.

I.E. extremely small stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With the letter, there's just enough here for an upvote. I like that they experimented on the suicidal guy and basically granted him what he wanted. Do think there could be a little more diversity in the folks, but I suppose it depends on what was ordered.

But why is the guy with the Nordic name speaking Old English?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems like a misuse of "statistically significant" - a better phrase would be something like "abnormally high". It sounds as though you want to imply that nanofilaments extended throughout neural matter. However, the Foundation would have no difficulty imaging these.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your comment, and I am glad you enjoyed reading it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn. Good catch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this one was great. Kept me engaged and interested the entire time. You have your + 1. No issues with the tone or spelling that I could see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because -4 stated that his name was "Unknown"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, why is only one "unknown" italicized?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for bringing it to my attention, Cubey. I have switched around a few of the personas to attempt to make it less stereotypical; after all, men in the 18-25 bracket are the most likely to suffer mental health problems leading to suicide.

I'll also add the backstory of -2 as a bonus, if you like. In my head she is from a time before humanity ever left Africa - so she doesn't speak any language which the Foundation could possibly know, and she's absolutely petrified. This is probably a time which is quite far removed from the time of the customer, but this customer specified quite a lot of specific parameters for her - hence the algorithm having to travel quite a long way before it found someone to fit. The one thing they forgot to mention is 'make her come from my time'!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this; it rather reminds me of Dollhouse, for obvious reasons. The concepts are different enough for me to not be bothered, and in particular I like the way certain information has been carefully left out - for example, the reason for the discrepancies noted in the footnote. Overall, this definitely gets an upvote from me; it's not a particularly complex or difficult concept, but it's well-written and engaging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your response.
After re-reading, my initial comments may have been too harsh. I'm removing my downvote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm curious about what you think I could add. I feel I know this one inside out, so I could probably add more pretty easily. Any thoughts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They are custom-made bodies. You'll find that the woman is not butch at all, but rather 'athletic looking' - which implies slim but fit. The appearances are all based on people I know (and in some cases also the personalities). The fact that the non-Caucasians die is entirely co-incidental. -2 survived until a very recent iteration, when I needed another one to die during testing, and as the one with whom communication was least possible, she made sense.

Allow me to explain -6, as an example of how I believe I am not being offensively stereotypical. Remember that these are returned items. They're the ones with whom the customers were not satisfied. So a man decided he liked the idea of lesbians, and thought it would be cool to have one as his slave/girlfriend. He doesn't specify much about her beyond 'lesbian' and 'fit'. What he doesn't realise is that lesbians, by definition, do not want to have sex with men. He happens to get a woman who is particularly damaged in her past relationships with men (spoiler: this is based very heavily on someone close to me) and she suspects, every time she's turned on and sees a man, that she has been kidnapped by this man for some nefarious purpose.

I apologise if I have come across in a way that has offended you. It was very much not my intention to, and indeed stereotyping is something I myself suffer and consider very harmful. You are correct to point out that it is something of a lazy and harmful notion to have the Japanese man killing himself. I needed someone who was going to commit suicide every time, I noticed I didn't have any East Asian instances yet, so my mind jumped to Japanese. I will consider making a change to that once I get home (on a train at the moment).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

My speculation, as ported over from the drafts forum

+ Spoiler-ish Speculation
- end Speculation

Am I correct in assuming that this thing is grabbing a different 'Soul' every time? IE: The chips are essentially embedded with search parameters - and are grabbing souls within those parameters and interfacing them with the body. - However, once released, the soul no longer matches, so the next time it's activated it's grabbing a different version of that soul - which accounts for the different memories - as those particular memories are outside the defined search parameters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not offended. You have the right to receive answers to your questions from authors themselves; it's just that I fin it evident that someone wanted a Japanese samurai that kills himself just because they were either massive assholes or really, really weird. For instance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No offense intended, but I'd rather the author answer this.

… Or so I was going to say at first, but then I realized that it won't really change anything. Using stereotypes in writing can be useful, but not when you overdo it - and this SCP is overdoing it. So, my downvote remains.

Also, the non-Caucasian specimens were both killed during testing but the others remain alive despite (in some cases) the same level of cooperation or usefulness. Hmm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. Feels like bog-standard custom-made people thing. Well-written, but the idea feels kind of stale to me. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was deliberate. They are custom made people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The skips are offensively stereotypical - a Japanese person who instantly wants to commit suicide, a butch-looking woman who HATES ALL MEN GRRR, androgynous man who is totally subservient to everyone, etc.

Whether this is deliberate (because they're supposed to be artificially made stereotypical people) or just bad writing is unknown by me for now, but unless clarified I'll assume the latter. Thus, -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

It's a little lacking in substance but overall an interesting idea I can get behind.

+ Spoiler
- Hide

Looks like these are returned rejects to a business that sells partially artificial pet humans for whatever purpose the buyer wants. My guess is that Mr. Subservient was ordered as sex slave, while Mr. Tumors was likely for medical testing. Others might be for historical posterity. The article is purposefully ambiguous as to whether these are actual people's souls ripped from their timelines, or just facsimiles. Like is future kid REALLY from the future, or just programmed to act like he is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am sorry about that, Smapti. Maybe one day I'll write something you like :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clarified that, thank you.

There ought to be sufficient information for you to figure out some of the why, when, and how. It's not exactly a resurrection service.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(…) although those affecting appearance (hair and nail growth, aging, etc) do not appear to be functioning.

You mean production of keratin-based structures and physical aging in general, or just hair and nail growth and exterior aging? I mean, do their brains or their lungs age, for instance? There is one that develops tumors, after all…

Otherwise, is it a resurrection service? Looks like it. Why, when and how are questions for another day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remain largely ambivalent about this. It's technically sound and I don't see any tonal errors, but it just doesn't do anything for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New SCP. Thanks to Athena_Grey, Riemann, Azzleflux, and ksaid for the feedback they provided which helped get this to the state in which it is today.
